questions.text: I feel that building consensus creates stronger momentum in the long run.
I find that I know things about people's inner states that they have not explicitly shared.
Small moments — a sunset, a kind word — can produce a surprisingly intense reaction in me.
I believe that the willingness to take suffering seriously — to let it weigh on me — is an expression of who I fundamentally am.
I tend to focus on whether an idea is good rather than on how it was received by the group.
I think about what needs to happen three steps ahead of where I currently am.
I notice that I don't carry the physical tension patterns that many people associate with stress.
I find myself naturally framing situations as problems for me to solve.
I believe that every act of generosity, no matter how small, matters more than any personal gain.
My emotional system operates with a kind of quiet competence that I've come to trust.
I prepare for meetings by reviewing agendas and relevant materials in advance.
I believe that maintaining a measured, objective posture during emotionally intense events is a form of service that is rarely recognized but always valuable.
I find that maintaining emotional equilibrium during painful situations allows me to focus on what needs to happen rather than on how the situation makes me feel.
I notice when a text message carries a tone that differs from the sender's usual style.
I have been praised for my ability to stay rational when emotions run high.
I take pride in being the driving force behind a project's success.
I feel that my personal achievements are among the things I am most proud of.
I learn whatever draws my curiosity without mapping it to identified skill gaps.
I experience emotions at a high volume — when something hits me, it hits hard.
I find that my internal experience is often far more intense than what shows on my face.
I do not usually notice when someone edits their story in real time based on audience reactions.
I tend to quietly handle background work that makes everyone else's contribution possible.
I believe that consistent small efforts compound into remarkable results over time.
I prefer that priorities be established through group discussion.
I prefer that important communications come from the group rather than a single person.
I find that my best ideas come when I abandon the plan and follow a sudden impulse.
I feel that building my own career and reputation is a central life priority.
I notice when the pitch of a room changes because one dominant personality entered or left.
I am inclined to make a choice and commit rather than keep options open.
I find that being the person others turn to in a crisis is one of the most meaningful roles I play.
I find that I can process upsetting news and continue functioning without much disruption.
I tend to allocate my vacation time to personal restoration and exploration.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the reality that reveals the seriousness of ethics in a way that no theoretical argument can match.
I do not pick up on when a social invitation is phrased to make declining difficult.
I'm comfortable starting with incomplete information and figuring things out along the way.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is the moral truth I hold most firmly, regardless of how inconvenient acting on it may be.
